perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not disclose Confidential In formation to any person or entity, except its employees or partners involved in the Project, or to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than fulfilling its obligations under the Project. Recipient acknowledges and agrees that breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by Recipient and/or anyone employed by or otherwise associated with Recipient may also constitute a violation of the Inside r Trading Act and/or other securities and other laws. Access to or disclosure of Confidential Information by Recipient shall be limited to those employees or partners covered by a then effective legal obligati on or agreement with Reci pient that prohibits the disclosure or unauthorized use of confidential in formation deemed confidential by Recipient ( Authorized Employees ). The existence of such an agreement with an employee or partner of the Recipient shall not operate to relieve the Recipient fro m liability for acts of such employee or partner that result in a breach of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall endeavor not to disclose Confidential Information to any person or entity, except perhaps its employees or partners involved in the Project, and should generally avoid using the Confidential Information for purposes other than fulfilling its obligations under the Project. Recipient acknowledges that a breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by Recipient and/or anyone employed by or otherwise associated with Recipient may, under certain circumstances, constitute a violation of the Insider Trading Act and/or other securities and other laws. Access to or disclosure of Confidential Information by Recipient may be extended to employees or partners that have some form of agreement with Recipient that addresses the disclosure or unauthorized use of confidential information deemed confidential by Recipient (Authorized Employees). The existence of such an agreement may reduce the liability of the Recipient for acts of such employee or partner that result in a breach of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity by replacing 'shall not disclose' with 'shall endeavor not to disclose' and 'should generally avoid,' weakening the prohibition on disclosure. The statement about Insider Trading Act is now watered down by introducing the phrase 'under certain circumstances'. Further, the mitigation of liability section provides even more ambiguity to this critical section.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Rule 10b-5, which prohibits deceptive practices and insider trading. By weakening the language around non-disclosure and suggesting that a breach 'may, under certain circumstances, constitute a violation,' the agreement implies that insider trading is not always a violation. This is in direct contradiction to the strict liability and broad prohibitions of Rule 10b-5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text creates a strong obligation for non-disclosure of confidential information, but the changed text significantly weakens this obligation. The original text states 'Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information,' while the changed text states 'Recipient shall endeavor not to disclose Confidential Information' and 'should generally avoid using the Confidential Information.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the prohibition on disclosure. The changed text also weakens the statement about Insider Trading Act by introducing the phrase 'under certain circumstances'. This modification contradicts the essence of Rule 10b-5, which aims to prevent deceptive practices. Specifically, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 (a) makes it unlawful 'To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By suggesting that a breach 'may, under certain circumstances, constitute a violation,' the agreement implies that insider trading is not always a violation which contradicts the broad prohibitions of Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not make copies of the Confid ential Information except for those copies required for use by Authorized Employees in the performance of the Agreement. Each copy, including its storage media, shall be marked Confidential, and also in clude all copyright, tradem ark and other proprietary notices which appear on the original . The Recipient agrees that all C onfidential Information, including any copies thereof, shall be returned to NASDAQ OMX or destroyed within ten (10) days of the expiration or other termination of the Project.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient should generally avoid making copies of the Confidential Information except when copies are needed by Authorized Employees in the performance of the Agreement. Copies, including their storage media, should preferably be marked Confidential, and it is suggested that all copyright, trademark and other proprietary notices which appear on the original also be included. The Recipient agrees to make an effort to return all Confidential Information, including any copies thereof, to NASDAQ OMX or destroy them within approximately ten (10) days of the expiration or other termination of the Project.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity by replacing 'shall not make copies' with 'should generally avoid,' 'shall be marked' with 'should preferably be marked,' and 'agrees that all...shall be returned' with 'agrees to make an effort to return.' This makes the obligations related to copying and returning/destroying confidential information less stringent and more discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes weaken the obligation to protect confidential information and return or destroy it after the project's termination. This could contradict New York Penal Law § 156.30, which addresses offenses involving unauthorized use of computer material. By creating ambiguity around the handling and destruction of confidential data, the modified clause may create circumstances where unauthorized copying or retention of data goes unaddressed, potentially constituting a violation of the law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with New York Penal Law § 156.30, which addresses unauthorized use of computer material. The original text used the terms 'shall not,' 'shall,' and 'agrees that...shall,' indicating mandatory actions. The modified text uses 'should generally avoid,' 'should preferably,' and 'agrees to make an effort,' making the obligations discretionary. This shift could create a situation where unauthorized copying or retention of data goes unaddressed, violating the law. The scraped law snippet 1 references this law, specifically mentioning "Unlawful duplication of computer related material in the first degree." The weakening of obligations directly undermines the intent of this law to prevent unauthorized copying and use of computer data.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Recipient receives a subpoena or other validly issued administrative or judicial process requesting Confidential Information, Recipient shall provide prompt actual notice to the General Counsel of NASDAQ OMX of such receipt, providing the Corporations with a reasonable opportunity to intervene in the proceeding before the time that Recipient is required to comply with such subpoena or other process. In the event that NASDAQ OMX instructs Recipient not to comply with any portion or all of the process, Recipient shall comply with such instructions and NASDAQ OMX shall indemnify and hold harmless the Recipient from any damages to th e extent that Recipient's damages result from its compliance with NASDAQ OMX's instructions.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Recipient happens to receive a subpoena or other administrative or judicial request for Confidential Information, the Recipient will attempt to provide notice to the General Counsel of NASDAQ OMX of such receipt, allowing the Corporations an opportunity to potentially intervene in the proceeding before the Recipient complies with the subpoena or other process. If NASDAQ OMX suggests that Recipient not comply with any portion or all of the process, Recipient may consider such suggestions, and NASDAQ OMX might indemnify and hold harmless the Recipient from some damages that could result from its consideration of NASDAQ OMX's suggestions.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications significantly weaken the obligation to notify NASDAQ OMX of a subpoena and to comply with their instructions. By replacing 'shall provide prompt actual notice' with 'will attempt to provide notice,' 'providing the Corporations with a reasonable opportunity' with 'allowing the Corporations an opportunity to potentially intervene,' 'Recipient shall comply with such instructions' with 'Recipient may consider such suggestions,' and 'NASDAQ OMX shall indemnify' with 'NASDAQ OMX might indemnify,' the clause becomes significantly less binding and introduces considerable uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: By suggesting the recipient 'may consider' NASDAQ OMX's instructions instead of strictly complying, and by weakening the indemnification language, the modified clause potentially encourages non-compliance with a validly issued subpoena. This conflicts with FRCP 45, which outlines the procedures for issuing and complying with subpoenas. While the recipient ultimately remains responsible for their own legal compliance, the encouragement to disregard instructions and the uncertain indemnification creates an environment where non-compliance is more likely, potentially violating FRCP 45 and related laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text creates an ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of compliance with a subpoena, potentially conflicting with FRCP 45. The original text used 'shall,' a mandatory term, regarding providing notice and complying with instructions. It also contained a clear indemnification clause. The changed text replaces these with weaker, non-committal language like 'will attempt,' 'potentially intervene,' 'may consider,' and 'might indemnify.' This introduces ambiguity about the recipient's obligations and NASDAQ OMX's responsibilities, making the agreement less binding and creating a loophole for non-compliance with legal processes. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight this weakening, and while the snippets don't directly address these changes, the introduction of ambiguities in place of mandatory actions does not align with the serious intent for a subpoena to be followed as described in snippet 1. Therefore, the modified contract does create a contradiction.
